What it means: The average of the normalized counts for a gene across all samples.
Why it matters: It filters out "noise." Genes with very low baseMean (e.g., < 10) are often excluded because their signals are too weak to be statistically reliable.

What it means: The effect size. It’s the $log_2$ ratio of the "dex" group over the "untreated" group.
Why it matters: of the "dex" group over the "untreated" group.The direction of change. A value of 2 means the gene is 4x more expressed in dex ($2^2 = 4$). A value of -1 means it is half as expressed ($2^{-1} = 0.5$).

What it means: The Standard Error of the log2FoldChange estimate.
Why it matters: It represents uncertainty. If a gene has a high fold change but a huge lfcSE, it means the samples in that group were inconsistent.

What it means: The Wald statistic (calculated as log2FoldChange / lfcSE).This is the "raw score" used to determine the p-value. The further this is from zero, the more likely the change is real.
The raw probability that the observed change happened by random chance.
Why it matters: It’s the traditional threshold ($P < 0.05$), but in genomics, it's dangerous to use on its own because you are testing 20,000+ genes at once.

What it means: The Adjusted P-value (often using Benjamini-Hochberg FDR).The "Golden Metric."
Why it matters: padj This corrects for false positives. You should almost always use padj < 0.05 (or $0.1$) to define your "Significant Genes."
